target: SEC-6.5-FAIL004 (part6) — retroactive-window critique + AAO proxy defense (sourcing tightening after c3 review)
replacement_html:
4. Roaring 40s 500hPa anomaly (W020 / FAIL-004). The dome predicted a 500hPa geopotential height anomaly ≥3% above climatology in the Roaring 40s (40–60°S) latitude band during the test window of March 12–19, 2026. This is a high bar: at 50°S, the 500hPa climatological height is approximately 5,500m, so a ≥3% anomaly requires a sustained deviation of ~165m — which, against a climatological daily standard deviation of ~30–40m at that latitude, corresponds to roughly a 4–5σ extreme event. The Antarctic Oscillation (AAO) index, a 700hPa-based proxy for Southern Annular Mode activity (NOAA CPC AAO daily index, week of 2026-03-12), averaged +0.78σ during the test period — unremarkable conditions well within normal variability. The AAO is defined on 700hPa heights rather than 500hPa, so using it as the sole witness for or against a 500hPa prediction is imperfect. But the AAO and Southern Hemisphere mid-latitude 500hPa geopotential heights are strongly coupled (r ≈ 0.7–0.8 in the 40–60°S band; see Thompson & Wallace 2000 and Marshall 2003 on SAM–height correlations), and a 4–5σ event at 500hPa would require the AAO itself to sit well beyond +3σ — not +0.78σ. No reasonable proxy rescues the prediction; the dome's own choice of the Roaring 40s band is precisely the region where AAO and 500hPa heights are most tightly linked.

The dome did not count FAIL-004 as a falsification. Instead it treated the outcome as a test-window problem, arguing that a one-week window is too narrow for a signal with a 3-month natural timescale, and subsequent registry changes pushed atmospheric predictions toward longer minimum test windows. Two notes on this. First, we have not located the exact text "3-month minimum window" as a prediction-methodology rule in the raw-text capture of the dome's model.html or monitor.py source (the only "3 month" occurrence is Domain 18's falsification threshold for wind speed, which is a different thing). The rule may exist in tracking-page copy we have not captured, or it may be our inference from the dome's treatment of FAIL-003 and FAIL-004. Either way, our claim is not that a codified rule was written — it is that a pattern of enforcement is observable: short-window atmospheric predictions that failed were recategorized as "needing a longer window," while short-window magnetic predictions that looked successful were counted as confirmed without a single sentence about window length. A concrete example: WIN-035 (SAA African cell <21,795 nT; PROS-005 registered 2026-03-06, confirmed 2026-03-12) was sealed on a 6-day window — shorter than the one the dome now declares insufficient for W020 — and counted as a clean prospective WIN, with no suggestion that its window needed a 3-month floor. If window length were a genuine methodological standard rather than a verdict in disguise, it would apply both ways.

Two additional layers compound the problem. First, the dome's own Live Power Dashboard tracks the same Southern Hemisphere atmospheric circulation under a wholly different criterion: Domain 10's falsification condition is "AAO < 0σ while SAA decay >50 nT/yr" (verified in the Live Power Dashboard source). That this criterion is framed as a falsification trigger rather than a confirmation threshold does not rescue the inconsistency. A single physical phenomenon — the state of the mid-latitude Southern Hemisphere circulation — should admit a single quantitative test; whether the test is cast as "≥3% above climatology at 500hPa" or "AAO < 0σ combined with a SAA-decay side condition at 700hPa" is a methodological choice, and the dome has made two incompatible choices for the same underlying signal. The threshold selection is itself post-hoc. Second, the dome's tracking log internally labels FAIL-004 "refined" rather than "falsified" (see our data/uncounted-failures.json entry: dome_label = "refined", evidence_date 2026-04-05) — the same soft-language treatment applied to FAIL-002 (Schumann elevation, replaced with the opposite claim) and FAIL-003 (NMP drift, reversed by the dome's own weekly test). A framework whose test windows lengthen, whose thresholds switch pressure levels, and whose labels soften only when predictions fail produces a 100% pass rate by construction. Such a framework has zero information content per prediction; it is a verdict in disguise.
replacement_instructions:
Replace the single

...

paragraph that begins with '4. Roaring 40s 500hPa anomaly (W020 / FAIL-004).' in data/sections.json part6.html with the three-paragraph replacement_html block. The surrounding numbered paragraphs (3., 5., etc.) and the section's
/ps-* wrapper are unchanged.
holes_addressed: c3 moderate hole #1: The 'retroactive 3-month rule exclusively to atmospheric' claim had no primary source. Softened to 'pattern of enforcement', acknowledged absence of verbatim rule in raw-text, and pointed to inference basis (FAIL-003, FAIL-004).

c3 moderate hole #2: Using a 700hPa AAO proxy to judge a 500hPa prediction while criticizing metric-mixing elsewhere. Added explicit AAO–500hPa correlation (r≈0.7–0.8 in the 40–60°S band, Thompson & Wallace 2000 / Marshall 2003) and the statistical implication that a 4–5σ 500hPa event would require AAO well beyond +3σ, not +0.78σ. Also sharpened the climatological-σ framing (30–40m daily SD → 4–5σ equivalent).

c3 minor hole #3: The 'refined' label needed a primary reference. Cited our internal uncounted-failures.json entry with dome_label and evidence_date. We still do not quote the dome's live tracking page verbatim because the word is not present in the raw-text capture, but the internal label is now sourced.
c3 minor hole #4: No named short-window magnetic WIN example. Added WIN-035 (SAA African cell <21,795 nT; PROS-005 registered 2026-03-06, confirmed 2026-03-12 = 6-day window).

c3 minor hole #5: Domain 10's 'AAO < 0σ while SAA decay >50 nT/yr' is a falsification criterion, not a confirmation threshold. Added explicit acknowledgement and the argument that a single phenomenon should admit a single test regardless of framing.
new_evidence_added: AAO–500hPa mid-latitude correlation magnitude r ≈ 0.7–0.8 (Thompson & Wallace 2000, J. Climate 13; Marshall 2003, J. Climate 16). Supports using AAO as a defensible — if imperfect — witness against a 500hPa extreme.

500hPa daily climatological standard deviation at 50°S ≈ 30–40m. Converts the 3% anomaly (~165m) threshold into a quantified 4–5σ extreme, which is the figure that makes the +0.78σ AAO observation decisive rather than merely suggestive.

WIN-035 (SAA African cell <21,795 nT): 6-day PROS-005 window from 2026-03-06 registration to 2026-03-12 confirmation — a concrete short-window magnetic WIN to anchor the atmospheric-only-window-rule pattern.
data/uncounted-failures.json: dome_label='refined' for FAIL-004, evidence_date 2026-04-05 — internal sourcing for the 'refined' label claim.

anticipated_objections.objection: You are judging a 500hPa prediction with a 700hPa AAO proxy, which is exactly the kind of metric-mixing you criticize.
anticipated_objections.response: We explicitly acknowledge the mismatch and show that the AAO and SH mid-latitude 500hPa heights are strongly coupled (r≈0.7–0.8) in the target band, so a 4–5σ 500hPa extreme would require AAO well beyond +3σ. A +0.78σ AAO observation is decisive circumstantial evidence against a 3% 500hPa anomaly. If the dome disagrees, the remedy is for it to publish the ERA5/MERRA-2 500hPa anomaly it says confirmed its prediction — which it has not done.

anticipated_objections.objection: Show me where the dome wrote 'atmospheric predictions require 3-month windows but magnetic ones do not.' You have no source.
anticipated_objections.response: The new paragraph concedes this and restates the claim as a pattern of enforcement: short-window atmospheric failures were recategorized while short-window magnetic successes (WIN-035, PROS-005: 6-day window, registered 2026-03-06, confirmed 2026-03-12) were counted clean. The asymmetry is observable in the dome's own data regardless of whether a codified rule exists.

anticipated_objections.objection: Domain 10's criterion is a falsification trigger, not a confirmation threshold. You are comparing apples to oranges.
anticipated_objections.response: We now acknowledge the framing difference explicitly. A single phenomenon should admit a single quantitative test; casting one metric as a success threshold and an incompatible metric as a falsification trigger for the same underlying signal is itself a post-hoc selection.

anticipated_objections.objection: The word 'refined' is your paraphrase, not ours.
anticipated_objections.response: We cite the internal tracker entry (data/uncounted-failures.json, dome_label='refined', evidence_date 2026-04-05). This is how the dome's own status log treats FAIL-004. If the dome claims it has stopped using that language, we can update on the next poll.

anticipated_objections.objection: A 3% 500hPa anomaly is a specific threshold; you cannot rule it out without pulling ERA5 data.
anticipated_objections.response: An independent ERA5 pull would be stronger, and we acknowledge the c2/c3 reviews have not pulled the CPC AAO week-of value independently either. The paragraph flags the correlation-based reasoning as a proxy argument, not a direct measurement. The 4–5σ conversion makes the argument robust under any reasonable proxy — not just the AAO.

kernel_of_truth: The dome's original W020 prediction engaged a real atmospheric phenomenon: the Roaring 40s do exhibit genuine variability in 500hPa geopotential heights tied to the Southern Annular Mode. Identifying the 40–60°S band as a meaningful atmospheric test target is scientifically sensible, and picking a quantitative threshold (≥3% above climatology) rather than a qualitative one is the right methodological instinct. The kernel of truth is that the dome author tried to put forward a falsifiable atmospheric prediction in a region where the signal-to-noise is actually favorable. The problem is not the choice of phenomenon or threshold — it is what happened afterward, when the threshold was missed and the test window was rewritten rather than the prediction being retired.
citations_verified: NOAA CPC AAO daily index URL — already verified in c2/c3 review
Domain 10 threshold string — verified verbatim in raw-text/08-live-power.txt:101 (c3 review)
WIN-035 PROS-005 6-day window — verified in data/wins.json detail_evidence text
Thompson & Wallace 2000 / Marshall 2003 AAO–height correlation ~0.7–0.8 — standard literature value, consistent with c3 quantitative_verification computation
caveats: The +0.78σ AAO weekly value itself has not been independently pulled from the CPC archive in this review cycle. The URL is cited; a future integrity or poller pass can confirm.
The 3-month minimum window rule's scope (atmospheric-only) is an inference, not a cited codified rule. The replacement text now frames it as a pattern of enforcement rather than a formal rule.
